hero.tagline: Colorado's small claims court offers a $7,500 cap with streamlined procedures for quick resolutions.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Lawyers at trial
at_a_glance.detail: Generally not allowed unless the defendant hires one

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Must file within 14 days of judgment

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

annual_filing_cap.notes: No more than 18 claims per year per county

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $7,500, you must waive the excess amount to file in small claims court; splitting a claim into multiple cases is not permitted.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Colorado allows you to file claims for breaches of contract and money owed up to $7,500. The statute of limitations varies by claim type.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend someone $1,000, and they failed to repay it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Gather any evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned a friend $2,500 with the understanding they'd repay in six months, but they haven't.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the loan agreement and any repayment attempts.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $5,000 and failed to make the agreed payments.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from default. Present the original promissory note and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided $3,000 worth of consulting services, but the client hasn't paid the invoice.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from when payment was due. Include the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $1,200 on their account with your business.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from last payment or charge. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $2,000 in unpaid rent and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from when rent was due. Bring the lease agreement and records of unpaid rent.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid with a $500 check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from the date of the check. Provide the original check and bank notice of insufficient funds.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $20 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 3x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $4,000 loan, and the primary borrower defaulted, leaving you responsible.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from default. Present the loan agreement and evidence of your payments.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Colorado's small claims court handles various consumer disputes up to $7,500, with specific statutes of limitations depending on the claim.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,000 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to refund or replace it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from purchase. Bring the receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged $500 but left your home in poor condition.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from service date. Provide the service agreement, photos, and correspondence.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,000 for repairs that didn't fix the issue.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from repair date. Bring repair invoices, receipts, and any warranties.

claims.name: Contractor failed to complete work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $5,000 to renovate your bathroom, but they abandoned the project halfway.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Provide the contract, payment records, and photos of incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver goods/services
claims.example: You paid $1,500 for catering services, but the vendor never showed up.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from event date. Bring the contract, payment proof, and any communication.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month gym membership, but they continued to charge your card.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from unauthorized charge. Provide cancellation notice and bank statements.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $6,000 that broke down within a week, and the seller refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from purchase. Bring the bill of sale, any warranties, and repair estimates.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A retailer advertised a product as new for $800, but it was refurbished.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from purchase. Provide the advertisement, receipt, and product details.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages plus attorney fees and costs; possible treble damages for bad faith.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Claims for property damage up to $7,500 can be filed in Colorado's small claims court, with a 3-year statute of limitations.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver caused $4,000 in damage to your car and refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from accident date. Bring the police report, repair estimates, and photos.

claims.name: Neighbor damaged your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell and caused $2,500 in damage to your fence.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos, repair estimates, and any communication.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left $3,000 worth of damage upon moving out.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery. Bring the lease agreement, photos, and repair invoices.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog dug up your garden, causing $1,200 in damage.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from incident. Provide photos, repair estimates, and witness statements.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left a $2,000 watch for repair, and it was returned damaged.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from return date. Bring the service agreement and photos of damage.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Monetary disputes between landlords and tenants up to $7,500 are eligible for small claims court in Colorado.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,500 security deposit without justification.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement and move-out inspection report.
claims.damage_boost: Treble damages if deposit wrongfully withheld.

claims.name: Failure to provide itemized deductions from deposit
claims.example: Landlord deducted $800 from your deposit without an itemized list.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease and any correspondence.
claims.damage_boost: Treble damages if deposit wrongfully withheld.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had no heat during winter, and the landlord refused to fix it.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from issue. Provide photos, repair requests, and any health department reports.

claims.name: Landlord retained tenant's personal property
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord kept your $2,000 bicycle.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from move-out. Bring the lease, move-out notice, and proof of property ownership.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing access to your apartment.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from eviction. Provide the lease, communication records, and any police reports.

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent after lease termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but the landlord didn't return the last month's rent after early termination.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease and payment records.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant owes $3,000 in unpaid rent and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from when rent was due. Bring the lease agreement and records of unpaid rent.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You paid $500 to fix a leaking roof after the landlord failed to address it and deducted it from rent.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from repair. Provide repair receipts and communication with the landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Employees can file claims for unpaid wages and related issues up to $7,500 in Colorado's small claims court.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 in unpaid wages for hours worked.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from due date. Provide pay stubs, time records, and any communication.
claims.damage_boost: Wages due plus penalty of 125% of unpaid wages or $250, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Employer failed to provide final paycheck
claims.example: After termination, your employer didn't give you your final paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from due date. Bring termination notice and records of hours worked.
claims.damage_boost: Wages due plus penalty of 125% of unpaid wages or $250, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in commissions, but your employer hasn't paid them.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from due date. Provide commission agreements and sales records.
claims.damage_boost: Wages due plus penalty of 125% of unpaid wages or $250, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime but weren't compensated at the overtime rate.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from due date. Bring time records and pay stubs.
claims.damage_boost: Wages due plus penalty of 125% of unpaid wages or $250, whichever is greater.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Individuals can file personal claims up to $7,500 in Colorado's small claims court, with varying statutes of limitations.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on an unmarked wet floor in a store and incurred $2,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from injury date. Provide medical records and incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You loaned a friend your $1,000 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from demand for return. Provide evidence of ownership and loan agreement.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: Someone stole your $3,000 bicycle, and you have evidence of their involvement.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from theft. Provide police reports and evidence of ownership.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: Vehicle-related disputes up to $7,500 can be addressed in Colorado's small claims court.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's repair work
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,500 for repairs that didn't fix the issue.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from repair date. Bring repair invoices and correspondence.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and you incurred $500 in fees.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from tow date. Provide towing receipts and photos of the parking area.

exclusions.explanation: Small claims court does not handle eviction cases; these must be filed in county court.
Claims involving defamation are excluded from small claims court jurisdiction.
Small claims court cannot issue orders requiring someone to do or stop doing something.
Divorce, custody, and related family law issues are not handled in small claims court.
Issues related to wills and estates are outside the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Small claims court cannot hear cases against federal entities.
Class action lawsuits are not permitted in small claims court.
Professional malpractice cases exceed the jurisdiction of small claims court.
These claims are handled by the Division of Workers' Compensation, not small claims court.
Bankruptcy cases are under federal jurisdiction and not handled in small claims court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in small claims court; however, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $20 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 3x check amount, whichever is greater.
Actual damages plus attorney fees and costs; possible treble damages for bad faith.
Treble damages if deposit wrongfully withheld.

statutory_multipliers.multiplier: 125% of unpaid wages or $250, whichever is greater
statutory_multipliers.explanation: Wages due plus penalty of 125% of unpaid wages or $250, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees may be recoverable in cases involving statutory violations, such as consumer protection or civil theft claims.
damages.postjudgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues at 8% per annum on unpaid judgments.

where_to_file.court_name: Small Claims Division of the County Court

filing_options: County where the defendant lives, works, or is a full-time student
County where the business has an office for transaction of business

venue_rules.scenario: Security deposit or restrictive covenant cases
filing_options: County where the property is located

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing availability depends on the county; check with the local court clerk.

frequent_filer.notes: No more than 18 claims per year per county

service_fees.notes: Contact local sheriff's office for exact fees.
Fees depend on current postal rates.

forms.name: Motion to File Without Payment and Supporting Financial Affidavit

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and may cover service fees; check with the court clerk.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees may be added to the judgment against the loser.

other_fees.name: Writ of execution
other_fees.notes: Post-judgment; contact court clerk for exact fees.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are typically added to the judgment.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge

entries.notes: Judgment can be revived before expiration.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy stays.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad check claims, a 30-day written notice demanding payment is required before filing suit.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: A written demand for unpaid wages is required; if not paid within 14 days, penalties may apply.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of deductions from the security deposit within 30 days of lease termination.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: A written demand is often required before filing a consumer protection claim to allow the business to address the issue.

forms.name: Notice, Claim, and Summons to Appear for Trial
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by notifying the defendant of the claim and trial date.

forms.name: Answer
forms.description: Allows the defendant to respond to the claim and present their side.

forms.name: Counterclaim
forms.description: Filed by the defendant if they have a claim against the plaintiff.

forms.name: Motion to File Without Payment and Supporting Financial Affidavit
forms.description: Request to waive filing fees due to financial hardship.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Filed to appeal a small claims court decision.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff or constable personally hands the papers to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail by clerk
methods.description: Court clerk sends documents via certified mail to the defendant.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A licensed individual delivers the documents to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent; if unavailable, serve an officer or manager.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Colorado rules and the rules of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, you may request permission for alternative service methods from the court.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document attempts and seek court approval for alternative methods.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer but must appear at the trial.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, the defendant may appeal within 14 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If the counterclaim exceeds $7,500, the case may be transferred to county or district court.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Attorneys are generally not allowed unless the defendant hires one; then the plaintiff may also hire one.
hearing.format: Bench trial with both parties presenting evidence and testimony; typically lasts 30-60 minutes.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter through the court clerk's office well in advance of the hearing.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within 7 days.
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some counties offer mediation services before the hearing; check with the local court.

appeals.type_notes: The appeal is a new trial in county court.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed within 14 days.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues at 8% per annum on unpaid judgments.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when debtor is W-2 employed at a stable job.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of garnishment, serve the bank, and the bank freezes and turns over funds from the debtor's account.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor maintains sufficient funds in bank accounts.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: File a transcript of judgment with the county recorder to place a lien on the debtor's real property.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns real property; lien must be satisfied upon sale or refinancing.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell debtor's non-exempt personal property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, have the sheriff seize non-exempt property, and sell it at public auction.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns valuable non-exempt personal property.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to revive the judgment before expiration; personal service on debtor required.

debtor_exam_form.name: Motion and Order for Hearing on Asset Disclosure

collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy may discharge the judgment; certain debts are non-dischargeable.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among multiple judgment creditors is generally determined by the order of filing or recording liens.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can appear without an attorney; a full-time officer or employee may represent the business.

non_attorney_representation_form.name: Notice, Claim, and Summons to Appear for Trial

fictitious_name_form.name: Notice, Claim, and Summons to Appear for Trial

businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses must use their legal entity name; trade names (DBAs) are not sufficient.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may represent themselves or have a full-time employee represent them.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face challenges in enforcing contracts; courts may deny recovery.

county_variations.detail: Denver County offers e-filing for small claims cases through the Colorado Courts E-Filing system.
El Paso County requires mediation for small claims disputes before proceeding to trial.

pitfalls.title: Exceeding the claim limit
pitfalls.what_happens: If your claim exceeds $7,500, you must waive the excess to proceed in small claims court; otherwise, the case may be dismissed or transferred.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Ensure your claim, excluding interest and costs, does not exceed $7,500 or be prepared to waive the excess amount.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to properly serve the defendant can result in delays or dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow the court's rules for service carefully, using approved methods and completing service within required timeframes.

pitfalls.title: Missing the statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will lead to dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type and file within the allowed period.

pitfalls.title: Failing to appear at trial
pitfalls.what_happens: If you do not appear at the scheduled trial, the court may dismiss your case or enter a default judgment against you.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Mark your calendar and ensure you attend the trial; if you cannot, notify the court in advance to request a reschedule.

pitfalls.title: Not collecting the judgment
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment does not guarantee payment; you are responsible for collection efforts.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Familiarize yourself with collection methods and be prepared to take necessary steps to enforce the judgment.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant files a counterclaim and you are unprepared, you could lose your case or owe money.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Review any counterclaims carefully and prepare your defense; consider seeking legal advice if needed.

recent_changes.title: Increase in small claims court limit
recent_changes.description: The jurisdictional limit for small claims court increased from $7,500 to $10,000, allowing for higher-value claims to be filed.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: In Colorado's small claims court, attorneys are generally not allowed unless the defendant hires one; then the plaintiff may also hire one.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: The typical timeline for a small claims case in Colorado ranges from 30 to 90 days from filing to resolution.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: You can sue for up to $7,500 in Colorado's small claims court.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear at the trial, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims court decision within 14 days by filing a Notice of Appeal.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Colorado is valid for 6 years and can be revived before expiration.

state_specific_notes: Colorado's small claims court has a jurisdictional limit of $7,500, excluding interest and costs.
Attorneys are generally not permitted in small claims court unless the defendant hires one.
The statute of limitations varies by claim type; for example, 3 years for written contracts and 2 years for personal injury.
Winning a judgment does not guarantee payment; plaintiffs are responsible for collection efforts.
Certain claims, such as evictions and defamation, are excluded from small claims court jurisdiction.

interesting_findings: Colorado allows for treble damages in cases of civil theft and wrongful withholding of security deposits.
The state imposes a limit on the number of small claims filings: no more than 18 per year per county.
Some counties in Colorado require mandatory mediation for small claims disputes before proceeding to trial.

sources.label: Colorado Judicial Branch - Small Claims Overview
Colo. Rev. Stat. § 13-6-403 (Jurisdiction of Small Claims Court)
